content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Precision used to be free. It isn’t anymore.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated with Gemini(Nano-Banana-2)Every token in your prompt has a price. You write prompts as if precision costs nothing, because for your entire life as a programmer, it didn’t.
Think about the economy you were trained in. The compiler demanded total precision and never once punished you for it. No type annotation ever made your program worse. No explicit lifetime ever confused the linker. You could be as specific as you wanted, about anything, forever, and the only possible outcomes were accepted or rejected with a line number.
Precision was free, so you learned to spend it everywhere.
That economy is gone the moment your reader is a language model. Because this reader doesn’t parse.
It drifts.
Not a member? Click the link. Worst case: you don’t read it. Best case: you stop arguing with LLMs.
Every token is a bid
A model doesn’t execute your prompt. It continues it. Each token you include shifts the probability of what comes next, whether or not you meant it to. There is no way to mark a token as “just context, ignore the connotations.”
